creator: wbe@psr.com
text:
(In reply to Stefan Eßer from comment #22)
> it did not follow the (communicated) requirement of commits to the project

> ... the calendar program shall only receive commits that address one bug or
> feature at a time. And each such commit needs to be accompanied by
> documentation that not only describes the change but gives a rationale for
> non-obvious changes.
Sorry, I wasn't aware that "each patch separately" was required.

> My issues with the patch file started with the initial chunk that introduces
> ```f_time_set```. This is a functional change that needs to be documented,
> and I do not see why it is required (it has not been for more than 30 years).
> An invalid time will make mktime return -1 and due to the removal of a test
> of ```f_time <= 0``` this case will not be detected and handled (the current
> code ignores the invalid date option, the patched code uses an unexpected
> constant).
["unexpected constant"??  No matter...]

1) The calendar.c line is Mktime, not mktime, but yes, day.c:Mktime uses mktime.
2) From the mktime/ctime(3) man page:

     Note that -1 is a valid result (representing one second before midnight
     UTC on the evening of 31 December 1969), so this cannot be relied upon to
     indicate success or failure; instead, tm_wday and / or tm_yday should be
     set to an out-of-bounds value (e.g. -1) prior to calling mktime() or
     timegm() and checked after the call.

and that was my problem with "if (f_time [from Mktime] <= 0)": calendar -t didn't work for dates before 1970 (not to mention that 0 is a valid time and the test should have been at least < 0, not <= 0).

Conclusion: Since -t only allows day.month.year and not hh:mm:ss, I'll grant that == -1, not (all results < 0), from Mktime indicates an error and modify the patch accordingly.

> there should be test cases that fail on the unpatched version and succeed on
> the patched version.
I'm not sure what kinds of tests you have in mind: something just for the person reading the bug report to see the problem, additional tests in calendar/tests/* (that compare results with regress.*), or something else?  The memcpy that should be memmove: I never tried to create something that evokes the bug, but memcpy(buf, buf+1, strlen(buf)) is clearly an overlapping copy.  The FSECSPERMINUTE (=60.0) that should be FMINSPERHOUR (=60.0) has no operational consequence, but is obviously wrong.  Some others are similarly obvious.

Patch format: It sounds like the format you want is each bug separately, consisting of a description, the patch for that bug, and whatever test(s) demonstrate the bug.  If all the patches are in one file, then concatenate them (first bug, second bug, ...).  Does that sound right, or do you want separate files (or patches here in the comments)?
